lecture_title: Impact Analysis, Software Processes & Continuous Integration

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the Impact Analysis phase?

options.text: Identifying the potential consequences of a change and estimating what needs to be modified to accomplish it
Locating the first class where the change must begin
Writing the new behaviour required by the change request
Confirming that all tests pass after the change is complete

questions.explanation: IA determines the strategy and impact of change: 'identifying the potential consequences of a change, or estimating what needs to be modified to accomplish a change.' B is Concept Location, C is Actualization, D is Verification. (AnalysisLab p.1; ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: Which phase immediately precedes Impact Analysis in the eight-step change process?
questions.explanation: IA sits after Concept Location and before Prefactoring. Initiation is two steps earlier; Actualization comes later. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: Which phase immediately follows Impact Analysis in the change process?
questions.explanation: IA is followed by Prefactoring, which prepares a clean landing site. Concept Location precedes IA; Verification runs alongside all phases; Conclusion is near the end. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: What does Impact Analysis take as its input and produce as its output?

options.text: Input: the initial impact set from Concept Location; output: the estimated impact set
Input: the estimated impact set; output: the initial impact set
Input: a change request; output: the located concept
Input: the estimated impact set; output: a verified baseline

questions.explanation: IA consumes the initial impact set (the seed classes from Concept Location) and grows it into the estimated impact set. B reverses the two; C describes Concept Location's I/O; D describes Conclusion/Verification. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: The classes identified during Concept Location form which set?
questions.explanation: Concept Location yields the initial impact set, the seed that IA expands. The estimated impact set is IA's output; the neighborhood is a graph notion; the product backlog is a process workproduct. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: Why is the result of Impact Analysis called the 'estimated' impact set?

options.text: It is a prediction of what will change, made before the code is edited, and may be wrong
It is calculated by a statistical estimator over historical commits
It estimates the monetary cost of the change in developer-hours
It is an approximation that is always larger than the true impact set

questions.explanation: The word 'estimated' signals it is a prediction made before editing; it can be too large or too small, and Verification later catches gaps. B/C invent mechanisms not in the deck; D is wrong because the estimate can be too small as well as too large. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: During Impact Analysis, how does the impact set grow from its initial state?

options.text: Class dependencies/interactions are analyzed and impacted classes are added to the set
All classes in the system are added, then unaffected ones are removed
Only the classes named in the change request text are added
Classes are added in alphabetical order until effort runs out

questions.explanation: IA analyzes interactions and adds impacted classes outward from the seed. B (whole-system then prune) is not the algorithm; C ignores propagation; D is nonsense. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: A student claims 'Impact Analysis finds the concept named in the change request.' Why is this wrong?

options.text: Finding the concept is Concept Location's job; IA expands the already-located concept into the full impact set
IA does find the concept, but only for bug fixes, not feature requests
IA finds the concept only after Prefactoring has cleaned the code
IA never deals with concepts; it works purely on bytecode

questions.explanation: The classic confusion: Concept Location finds where the change begins; IA takes that initial set and propagates outward. B/C/D mischaracterize the phase boundary. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: Which course artifact catches cases where the estimated impact set was wrong (too large or too small)?
questions.explanation: Because the impact set is only an estimate, Verification (the right-hand bar of the process diagram) is what catches mis-estimates. The other options serve other purposes. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: According to the deck, when do two classes interact?
questions.explanation: The deck's definition: two classes interact if they have something in common (a dependency or coordination). Same package, same commit, and shared superclass are neither necessary nor the deck's criterion. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: The deck gives two flavors of interaction between classes. What are they?
questions.explanation: Interactions come as a dependency (a contract: one depends on the other) or coordination (they share coding/schedule/dataflow). B/C/D are real OO terms but are not the deck's two interaction flavors. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: How does the deck define a dependency between two classes?

options.text: One depends on the other; there is a contract between them
They share the same coding, schedule, etc.
They are both modified in the same iteration
They have an undirected edge in the interaction graph

questions.explanation: A dependency is a directional contract (A depends on B). B describes coordination, not dependency; C is a process coincidence; D describes interactions generally, which are undirected. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: How does the deck define coordination between two classes?

options.text: They share the same coding, schedule, etc.
One depends on the other through a contract
They are deployed to the same server environment

questions.explanation: Coordination is when classes share the same coding, schedule, etc. B is a dependency; C/D are unrelated to the deck's definition. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: Why is bidirectionality the defining property of an interaction for Impact Analysis?

options.text: Change can propagate from A to B or from B to A, so IA cannot just follow dependency arrows one way
Because every dependency is automatically mutual in Java
Because the interaction graph stores each edge twice for performance
Because undirected graphs are easier to draw than directed ones

questions.explanation: Interactions propagate change in both directions, which is why IA walks the interaction graph rather than following dependency arrows one way. B is false (dependencies are directional); C/D are irrelevant rationalizations. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: In the coordination code example, class C runs 'b.paint(a.get());' inside foo(). What does this illustrate?

options.text: A dataflow between a and b, so a and b coordinate even though neither depends on the other's interface
That a depends on b through a direct method contract
That C is a propagating (mailman) class
That a and b are the same object

questions.explanation: The line creates a dataflow between a and b; the coordination lives in C's body, so a and b coordinate without either depending on the other's interface. B is wrong (no direct a-b contract); C confuses this with the mailman concept; D is false. (ImpactAnalysis p.8)

questions.stem: On which graph is Impact Analysis performed?
questions.explanation: IA is performed on the interaction graph because change propagates bidirectionally; the dependency graph only captures one-way arrows. Inheritance hierarchy and call stack are different structures. (ImpactAnalysis p.9)

questions.stem: How does the interaction diagram differ from the dependency diagram for the same classes C, A, B?

options.text: The interaction diagram uses undirected lines and adds a direct A–B line; the dependency diagram uses directed arrows from C down to A and B
The interaction diagram uses directed arrows; the dependency diagram uses undirected lines
Both use directed arrows, but the interaction diagram reverses them
They are identical; the names are interchangeable

questions.explanation: Dependency: dashed directed arrows C->A, C->B. Interaction: plain undirected lines C-A, C-B, plus a direct A-B line because A and B coordinate through C. B/C swap or distort this; D is false. (ImpactAnalysis p.9)

questions.stem: Formally, the class interaction graph is written G = (X, I). What are X and I?

options.text: X = the set of classes; I = the set of interactions (edges)
X = the set of interactions; I = the set of classes
X = the impacted classes; I = the unimpacted classes

questions.explanation: G = (X, I) with X the classes (vertices) and I the interactions (edges). B swaps them; C/D invent meanings. (ImpactAnalysis p.6)

questions.stem: The neighborhood of class A is defined as N(A) = { B | (A,B) ∈ I }. What does this set contain?

options.text: Every class that directly interacts with A
Every class A transitively depends on

questions.explanation: N(A) is exactly the classes sharing an edge with A in the interaction graph, i.e. those directly interacting with A. Transitive reach (B), package membership (C), and subclasses (D) are different notions. (ImpactAnalysis p.6)

questions.stem: In the 'Neighborhood of Item' picture (Store–Inventory, Store–Cashiers, Inventory–Item, Item–Price, Cashiers–CashierRecord), which classes are Item's neighbors?
questions.explanation: Item shares edges only with Inventory (Inventory–Item) and Price (Item–Price), so N(Item) = {Inventory, Price}. Store and Cashiers connect elsewhere; CashierRecord attaches to Cashiers. (ImpactAnalysis p.7)

questions.stem: What role does the neighborhood play in the IA algorithm?

options.text: When a class is found changed, its neighbors become the next candidates to inspect
It defines the set of classes that are never inspected
It is the final estimated impact set
It lists the classes that can be safely deleted

questions.explanation: The neighborhood is the unit the algorithm expands by: a changed (or propagating) class schedules its BLANK neighbors as NEXT. B/C/D misstate its role. (ImpactAnalysis p.6-7)

questions.stem: What is a propagating ('mailman') class in Impact Analysis?

options.text: A class that is neither the origin nor a final target of the change but relays it between neighbors
A class that originates the change request
A class that is never inspected during IA
A class that must be deleted to complete the change

questions.explanation: A propagating class relays change between neighbors without itself originating or being the final target. B is the seed class; C describes a BLANK class; D is unrelated. (ImpactAnalysis p.10)

questions.stem: In the John–mailman–Paul analogy, what does the mailman represent?

options.text: The propagating class that carries the change from John to Paul without changing itself
The class that originates the change
The Verification phase that checks the change

questions.explanation: John (origin) writes to Paul (target); the mailman carries the letter — the propagating class that relays change. John is the origin, not the mailman; the impact set and Verification are not actors in the analogy. (ImpactAnalysis p.10)

questions.stem: Why would marking a true mailman class as UNCHANGED be a mistake?

options.text: It would wrongly stop propagation, leaving its other neighbors uninspected even though change passes through it
It would cause the loop to never terminate
UNCHANGED classes still expand the frontier, so nothing would change
It would add the class to the estimated impact set incorrectly

questions.explanation: A mailman needs no code change yet still forces neighbors to be inspected; marking it UNCHANGED stops that propagation prematurely. The loop still terminates (B wrong); UNCHANGED does NOT expand the frontier (C wrong); UNCHANGED is excluded from the impact set, so D is the opposite error. (ImpactAnalysis p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: A propagating class itself needs no new code. Why is it still significant to IA?

options.text: Change passes through it, so its BLANK neighbors must still be scheduled for inspection
It must be rewritten from scratch
It is always the seed of the impact set
It is removed from the interaction graph

questions.explanation: Even with no code change, a propagating class is on the change's path, so its neighbors must be inspected. B/C/D contradict the mailman concept. (ImpactAnalysis p.10)

questions.stem: Which mark means a class was never inspected and is not scheduled for inspection?
questions.explanation: BLANK = never inspected, not scheduled. NEXT = scheduled; UNCHANGED = inspected and not impacted; INSPECTED = inspected-but-not-changed in the interactive variant. (ImpactAnalysis p.11)

questions.stem: Which mark means a class is currently scheduled for inspection?
questions.explanation: NEXT = scheduled for inspection. BLANK is unscheduled; CHANGED/PROPAGATING are post-inspection results. (ImpactAnalysis p.11)

questions.stem: Which mark means programmers inspected the class and found it IS impacted by the change?
questions.explanation: CHANGED = inspected and impacted. UNCHANGED = inspected and not impacted; BLANK = not inspected; NEXT = scheduled. (ImpactAnalysis p.11)

questions.stem: Which mark means programmers inspected the class and found it is NOT impacted?
questions.explanation: UNCHANGED = inspected and not impacted. CHANGED = impacted; PROPAGATING = relays change; BLANK = not inspected. (ImpactAnalysis p.11)

questions.stem: Which two marks appear in the algorithm diagrams beyond BLANK/CHANGED/UNCHANGED/NEXT?
questions.explanation: PROPAGATING (mailman/relay case) and INSPECTED (interactive variant, in place of UNCHANGED at the loop-back) are the two extra marks. B/C/D are not used in the deck. (ImpactAnalysis p.12, p.16)

questions.stem: In the interactive IA variant, the INSPECTED mark is used in place of which other mark at the loop-back point?
questions.explanation: Interactive IA uses INSPECTED in place of UNCHANGED at the loop-back point. It does not replace CHANGED, NEXT, or BLANK. (ImpactAnalysis p.16)

questions.stem: A class is marked PROPAGATING. What does the algorithm do next?

options.text: Mark its BLANK neighbors as NEXT so the change relays onward
Stop, since propagating classes end the loop
Mark it UNCHANGED and remove it from the graph
Add all its neighbors directly as CHANGED without inspection

questions.explanation: PROPAGATING (like CHANGED) schedules its BLANK neighbors as NEXT, relaying the change. It does not stop the loop (B), is not converted to UNCHANGED (C), and never auto-marks neighbors CHANGED without inspection (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: What is the very first step of the IA marking algorithm?

options.text: Create the interaction diagram and mark all classes as BLANK
Mark the concept-location class as CHANGED
Select a NEXT class and inspect it

questions.explanation: Step 1 builds the interaction diagram and marks every class BLANK. Seeding CHANGED (B), scheduling NEXT (C), and inspecting (D) all come afterward. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: After marking all classes BLANK, what does the algorithm do to seed the impact set?

options.text: Mark the class located during Concept Location as CHANGED
Mark the largest class as PROPAGATING
Mark all leaf classes as UNCHANGED

questions.explanation: The seed is the concept-location class, marked CHANGED, which injects the initial impact set into the estimated set. The other options misstate the seeding step. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: After seeding the CHANGED class, which classes are scheduled as NEXT?

options.text: All BLANK classes in the graph

questions.explanation: Only the BLANK neighbors of changed classes become NEXT. Scheduling all BLANK classes (B) ignores locality; UNCHANGED neighbors (C) are already resolved; transitive-only (D) skips the immediate frontier. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: The IA loop repeatedly asks one question to decide whether to continue. What is it?

options.text: Are there any classes still marked NEXT?
Are there any classes still marked BLANK?
Has the effort budget been exhausted?
Have all classes been marked CHANGED?

questions.explanation: The loop guard is whether any classes are marked NEXT; if none remain, it stops. BLANK classes may legitimately remain unvisited; effort budget and all-CHANGED are not the termination test. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: When no classes remain marked NEXT, which classes constitute the estimated impact set?

options.text: All classes marked CHANGED or PROPAGATING

questions.explanation: On termination, the estimated impact set is the CHANGED plus PROPAGATING classes. UNCHANGED and BLANK classes are excluded; not every class is included. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Which marks do NOT extend the inspection frontier (do not schedule new NEXT classes)?
questions.explanation: Only CHANGED and PROPAGATING expand the frontier; UNCHANGED/INSPECTED classes are dead-ends. B is the opposite; C are pre-inspection states; D mixes a frontier-extender (PROPAGATING) with a dead-end. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Why is the IA marking algorithm guaranteed to terminate?

options.text: Each class moves BLANK to NEXT to a final mark (CHANGED/PROPAGATING/UNCHANGED/INSPECTED) at most once, and the graph is finite
Because UNCHANGED classes are deleted, shrinking the graph each pass
Because the effort budget caps the number of inspections
Because the interaction graph is always acyclic

questions.explanation: Termination follows from monotone, once-only mark transitions over a finite vertex set. Classes are not deleted (B); there is no effort cap in the algorithm (C); interaction graphs can contain cycles (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: When a selected NEXT class is inspected and found CHANGED, what happens?

options.text: It is marked CHANGED and its BLANK neighbors are marked NEXT
It is marked CHANGED and the loop stops
It is marked UNCHANGED and removed
Its neighbors are immediately marked CHANGED

questions.explanation: A CHANGED class spreads: its BLANK neighbors become NEXT. The loop does not stop on a single CHANGED (B); it is not converted to UNCHANGED (C); neighbors are scheduled for inspection, not auto-CHANGED (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: When a selected NEXT class is inspected and found UNCHANGED, what happens to the frontier?

options.text: It is marked UNCHANGED and does not expand the frontier
Its neighbors are scheduled as NEXT anyway
The whole graph is re-marked BLANK

questions.explanation: UNCHANGED ends that branch: no new NEXT classes are scheduled. It does not relay (B/C), and the graph is not reset (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: In the Interactive IA swimlane diagram, how is the work split between the Computer and the Programmer?

options.text: The Computer creates the diagram, marks BLANK, manages the NEXT queue and applies marks; the Programmer selects a NEXT class and decides INSPECTED/PROPAGATING/CHANGED
The Programmer manages the queue; the Computer decides each class's mark
Both run the full algorithm independently and compare results
The Computer does everything; the Programmer only reads the final impact set

questions.explanation: Interactive IA is human-in-the-loop: tooling tracks state and applies marks; the human supplies the judgement of each class. B reverses the roles; C/D misdescribe the division of labour. (ImpactAnalysis p.16)

questions.stem: What does the Interactive IA variant demonstrate about Impact Analysis as an activity?

options.text: It is human-in-the-loop: tooling tracks state, the programmer supplies judgement
It is fully automatable with no human input

questions.explanation: The swimlane split shows IA needs human judgement to classify each class while the tool manages bookkeeping. B contradicts the swimlane; C is false (the graph is central); D is unrelated. (ImpactAnalysis p.16)

questions.stem: Running IA on the store graph seeded at Item, suppose Inventory becomes CHANGED, Price UNCHANGED, Store PROPAGATING, Cashiers UNCHANGED, and CashierRecord is never reached. What is the estimated impact set?
questions.explanation: The estimated impact set is CHANGED plus PROPAGATING: Item (seed), Inventory (CHANGED), Store (PROPAGATING). Price and Cashiers are UNCHANGED; CashierRecord stays BLANK. (ImpactAnalysis p.10, p.7)

questions.stem: In that same store-graph walkthrough, why was Cashiers inspected at all even though it ended UNCHANGED?

options.text: Store was PROPAGATING, which scheduled its BLANK neighbor Cashiers as NEXT
Cashiers is a neighbor of Item and was seeded directly
Every class is always inspected exactly once
CashierRecord forced Cashiers to be inspected

questions.explanation: Store being PROPAGATING (the mailman) forced its neighbor Cashiers onto the frontier, which is the value of the propagating refinement. Cashiers is not Item's neighbor (B); not every class is inspected, e.g. CashierRecord stayed BLANK (C); CashierRecord did not drive it (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.10)

questions.stem: In the IA worked-example trace, what is the typical sequence of shadings a box passes through?

options.text: BLANK, then NEXT (hatched), then CHANGED (dark) or UNCHANGED (U)

questions.explanation: The trace shows boxes move BLANK to NEXT (hatched) to CHANGED (dark) or UNCHANGED (marked U). The other orderings reverse or scramble the lifecycle. (ImpactAnalysis p.13)

questions.stem: What is the relationship between the 'IA including propagating classes' diagram and the 'Interactive IA' diagram?

options.text: They are two presentations of the same algorithm; the interactive one splits the work into Computer and Programmer swimlanes
They are different algorithms producing different impact sets
The interactive one omits propagating classes entirely
The propagating one is for teams, the interactive one is for solo work

questions.explanation: Both diagrams encode the same marking loop; the interactive version just allocates steps between a Computer lane and a Programmer lane. They are not different algorithms (B), the interactive one still handles propagation (C), and neither is team-vs-solo specific (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.12, p.16)

questions.stem: Which lab task has students execute Rajlich's Figure 7.9 IA algorithm against a real codebase?
questions.explanation: AnalysisLab has students run the Figure 7.9 IA algorithm to produce an estimated impact set. CILab stands up a CI pipeline; the other labs belong to other lectures. (AnalysisLab p.1)

questions.stem: Besides finding impacted classes, what additional decision does Impact Analysis support?

options.text: Weighing alternative places to make the change
Choosing which programmer is assigned the change
Writing the unit tests for the change

questions.explanation: IA also weighs alternative locations for the change, surfacing the trade-offs. Assignment (B) and baselines (C) are process concerns; test-writing (D) belongs to later phases. (ImpactAnalysis p.14)

questions.stem: In the temperature example, a program displays Fahrenheit and must be changed to Celsius. How many candidate locations does IA identify?

options.text: Two: where sensor data is converted to temperature, and where temperature is displayed
Zero: the change is impossible without new sensors

questions.explanation: The deck names two candidate locations: the sensor-to-temperature conversion and the temperature-to-display site, and IA weighs them. B understates, C overstates, and D is false. (ImpactAnalysis p.14)

questions.stem: What are the two criteria IA uses to choose between alternative change locations?

options.text: Required effort of the change, and clarity of the resulting code
Lines of code added, and number of tests written
Coupling and cohesion of the classes

questions.explanation: The two criteria are required effort and clarity of the resulting code. B/C/D are not the deck's criteria. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: Why do the two IA criteria (effort vs clarity) often contradict each other in the Fahrenheit-to-Celsius example?

options.text: Patching the UI takes less effort but scatters temperature logic; converting centrally is more effort but clearer
Clearer code always takes less effort, so there is no real conflict
Effort and clarity are the same measurement under different names
The UI patch is both lower effort and clearer

questions.explanation: It is easier to just adjust the UI, but it is clearer to keep all temperature calculations in one place, so the criteria pull in opposite directions. B/C/D deny the genuine conflict. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: The conflict between the two IA criteria is described as a conflict between which two kinds of goals?
questions.explanation: The deck frames effort-vs-clarity as the conflict between short-term and long-term goals. The other dichotomies are not how the deck labels it. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: A teammate argues 'IA should always pick the lowest-effort change location.' Why is this an incorrect reading of the two criteria?

options.text: Least effort often hurts clarity; IA surfaces both options with their costs rather than mechanically minimizing effort
IA always picks the highest-effort option to maximize clarity
Effort is irrelevant once the impact set is known
IA never considers effort, only clarity

questions.explanation: The least-effort choice (patch the UI) often scatters logic and harms clarity; IA presents both options with costs so the programmer decides. B/C/D distort the balance between the two criteria. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: In the Fahrenheit-to-Celsius decision, which option favors long-term clarity at the cost of higher immediate effort?

options.text: Converting temperature centrally so all calculations live in one place
Changing only the user interface display
Adding a second display widget for Celsius
Leaving the code unchanged and documenting the difference

questions.explanation: Central conversion costs more now but keeps temperature logic in one place, improving long-term clarity and cheapening future changes. The UI-only patch (B) is the low-effort/low-clarity option; C/D are not the deck's alternatives. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: The same short-term-vs-long-term tension that the IA criteria expose also motivates which other course concepts?
questions.explanation: The effort-vs-clarity conflict is the same tension behind refactoring/prefactoring and the notion of technical debt. The other options are unrelated course topics. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: What does the right-hand bar labelled VERIFICATION on the change-process diagram signify relative to Impact Analysis?

options.text: Verification runs across all phases, including catching IA's mis-estimates
Verification replaces IA for small changes
Verification is a sub-step inside IA

questions.explanation: Verification spans the whole process and later catches places where the estimated impact set was wrong. It does not precede IA exclusively (B), replace IA (C), or sit inside IA (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: Which textbook chapter do the Impact Analysis slides attribute their material to?
questions.explanation: The IA deck cites Rajlich Ch. 7. Chapter 13 is cited by the Team Processes deck; Beck and Booch are CI-history references. (ImpactAnalysis p.8-9)

questions.stem: In the SB5-MAI labs, the Analysis Lab applies the IA marking algorithm to which target codebase?

options.text: A real Java codebase (in this course, the JHotDraw-based project)
The cash-register store program from the slides

questions.explanation: The cash-register names are pedagogical stand-ins on the slides; the lab runs the same mechanics over the real JHotDraw-based Java codebase. The store program is only illustrative (B); the lab uses real code not pseudocode (C); Bugzilla appears only as an interruption example (D). (AnalysisLab p.1)

questions.stem: What running example domain do the Lecture-3 slides actually use to illustrate interactions and processes?

options.text: A cash-register / store domain (Store, Inventory, Item, Price, Cashiers)

questions.explanation: None of the Lecture-3 decks name JHotDraw on a slide; the running example is the cash-register/store domain. JHotDraw is the lab's applied target, not the slide example. (ImpactAnalysis p.7-8)

questions.stem: In the Analysis Lab portfolio table, what does each student record after running concept location and IA?

options.text: Packages visited and the number of classes visited
The exact LOC of every changed file
The defect density of the codebase

questions.explanation: The lab's Table 1 tabulates package name, number of classes, and comments for the classes visited. LOC (B), defect density (C), and the Manifesto (D) are not what this table records. (AnalysisLab p.2)

questions.stem: The Analysis Lab notes that change impact analysis can rely on which two kinds of analysis?
questions.explanation: The lab frames IA in terms of static and dynamic analysis of the code. The other pairings are not what the lab describes. (AnalysisLab p.1)

questions.stem: Why can't Impact Analysis simply follow dependency arrows in one direction to find all impacted classes?

options.text: Change can ripple back up a dependency, so propagation is bidirectional and IA must use the undirected interaction graph
Dependency arrows are always wrong in practice
Following arrows would be too slow for large graphs
Dependencies and interactions are unrelated concepts

questions.explanation: Because a change to B can force its dependent A to change too, propagation runs both ways, so IA walks the undirected interaction graph. B is false; speed (C) is not the reason; dependencies are one flavor of interaction (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.5, p.9)

questions.stem: In the coordination example, where does the coordination between objects a and b actually live?

options.text: Inside the body of class C's method foo()
In the public interface of class A
In a shared superclass of a and b

questions.explanation: The dataflow b.paint(a.get()) is written in C's foo() body, so the coordination lives there even though a and b have no direct contract. The other locations are not where the deck places it. (ImpactAnalysis p.8)

questions.stem: According to the Intro to Software Processes deck, the study of software processes is described as what?

options.text: An optional add-on for large teams
The same thing as project accounting

questions.explanation: The deck states that the study of software processes is the core of software engineering. The other options contradict that framing. (IntroSwProcesses p.1)

questions.stem: Which slogan captures the deck's motivation for studying software processes?

options.text: Good process leads to good product

questions.explanation: The deck's slogan is 'Good process leads to good product.' The others are not the deck's message; D even inverts an Agile value. (IntroSwProcesses p.1)

questions.stem: What can you learn from studying SUCCESSFUL past projects, according to the deck?

options.text: The processes that worked well, yielding a prescription for future projects
Only the names of the developers involved

questions.explanation: Successful projects reveal processes that worked well, giving a prescription for the future; unsuccessful ones reveal the problems that led to failure. B/C/D miss the point. (IntroSwProcesses p.1)

questions.stem: Along which two axes does the deck say software processes vary?

options.text: Team (organization, collaboration, skills) and System (technology, domain, size, quality)

questions.explanation: Processes vary by Team (organization, collaboration, skills) and by System (technology, domain, size, expected quality). The other pairs are not the deck's two axes. (IntroSwProcesses p.2)

questions.stem: On the process granularity scale, which is the COARSEST level?
questions.explanation: Lifecycle (software life-span models) is the coarsest granularity. Task, subtask/phase, and step/action are progressively finer. (IntroSwProcesses p.3-4)

questions.stem: On the granularity hierarchy, which examples belong to the 'stage' level?
questions.explanation: The 'stage' level is illustrated by evolution and servicing. Staged/waterfall are lifecycles; SIP/AIP/DIP are processes; concept location/actualization are subtasks/phases. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: On the granularity hierarchy, at what level is 'software change' classified?
questions.explanation: A software change is a task (alongside acceptance testing). Processes are SIP/AIP/DIP; subtasks/phases are concept location/actualization; steps/actions are class inspections. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: On the granularity hierarchy, at what level are 'concept location' and 'actualization' classified?
questions.explanation: Concept location and actualization are subtasks/phases of the software-change task. The change itself is a task; SIP/AIP/DIP are processes; class inspection is a step. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: On the granularity hierarchy, 'inspection of a class' (exactly what IA does) sits at which level?
questions.explanation: Inspection of a class is a step/action, the finest level. Phases like IA are made up of such steps; the change is a task; SIP/AIP/DIP are processes. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: On the granularity hierarchy, where do SIP, AIP, and DIP sit?
questions.explanation: SIP, AIP, and DIP are processes, the level that repeats the change task. Lifecycle is coarser (staged/waterfall); task and step/action are finer. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: The deck says the word 'process' is usually reserved for activities that fit within roughly what scope?

options.text: A single stage or a few neighboring stages

questions.explanation: 'Process' usually denotes things fitting within a single stage or a few neighboring stages; life-span models are the coarsest. B/C/D misstate the scope. (IntroSwProcesses p.3)

questions.stem: Using the granularity hierarchy, how is Impact Analysis best classified, and out of what is it composed?

options.text: It is a phase composed of class-inspection steps
It is a task composed of several processes
It is a lifecycle composed of stages
It is a step composed of subtasks

questions.explanation: IA is a phase (subtask) of the software-change task, and it is built from class-inspection steps. B/C/D invert or scramble the levels. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: On the granularity hierarchy, which example is given for the 'lifecycle' level?
questions.explanation: Lifecycle is exemplified by staged and waterfall models. Evolution/servicing are stages; software change/acceptance testing are tasks; class inspection is a step. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: An exam answer labels 'acceptance testing' as a process. Why is that a granularity error?

options.text: Acceptance testing is a task, not a process; processes are SIP/AIP/DIP
Acceptance testing is a lifecycle, not a process
Acceptance testing is a step, not a process

questions.explanation: Acceptance testing is listed at the task level alongside software change; processes are SIP/AIP/DIP. It is neither a lifecycle (B) nor a single step (C), so D is wrong too. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: In the four forms of a process, what is the 'process model'?

options.text: The prescription/blueprint of what the tasks should be and how they fit together
The actual run of the process with deviations
The set of measures an observer collects

questions.explanation: The model is the blueprint: a prescription of the tasks and their arrangement. B is enactment, C is performance, D is plan. (IntroSwProcesses p.5)

questions.stem: What distinguishes the 'enactment' of a process from its model?

options.text: Enactment is the actual process as it runs, including inevitable deviations and exceptions
Enactment is the blueprint with no deviations
Enactment is only the cost measurements
Enactment is the plan for next quarter

questions.explanation: Enactment is the model as actually run, with inevitable deviations and exceptions (e.g. an unplanned tool install). The blueprint is the model (B); measures are performance (C); future expectations are the plan (D). (IntroSwProcesses p.5)

questions.stem: In the four forms of a process, what is 'performance'?

options.text: The set of measures an observer of an enacted process collects (time, cost, quality)
The decisions about how to enact the model in future

questions.explanation: Performance is the measurements (time, cost, quality) gathered while observing the enactment. The blueprint is the model (B); future decisions are the plan (C); deviations characterize enactment (D). (IntroSwProcesses p.6)

questions.stem: In the four forms of a process, what is the 'plan'?

options.text: The expected future performance and the decisions stakeholders take about how to enact the model

questions.explanation: The plan is expected future performance plus stakeholder decisions about enacting the model. Collected measures are performance (B); the blueprint is the model (C); the actual run is enactment (D). (IntroSwProcesses p.6)

questions.stem: The unplanned 'install new version of Bugzilla' interruption inside a worked task sequence is an example of which process form?
questions.explanation: Such an interruption is a real-world deviation, which characterizes enactment, not the idealized model. It is not a measurement (performance) or a future expectation (plan). (IntroSwProcesses p.5)

questions.stem: Order these from blueprint to future expectation: model, enactment, performance, plan. Which sequence matches the deck's definitions?

options.text: Model (blueprint), enactment (actual run), performance (measures), plan (expected future)

questions.explanation: The deck defines model = blueprint, enactment = actual run with deviations, performance = collected measures, plan = expected future performance. Only A keeps that mapping. (IntroSwProcesses p.5-6)

questions.stem: What is the Solo Iterative Process (SIP)?

options.text: A single programmer repeats software changes, adding functionality one step at a time
A large team integrating dozens of times per day
A manager-directed process with specialized roles
A process with a permission-to-commit safeguard

questions.explanation: SIP is one programmer repeating changes, adding functionality one step at a time. B/C/D describe team processes (CI/DIP/CIP), not SIP. (IntroSwProcesses p.7)

questions.stem: Why does the course teach SIP first, before team processes?

options.text: SIP demonstrates characteristics shared by all iterative processes
SIP is the only process used in industry
SIP eliminates the need for impact analysis

questions.explanation: SIP is taught first because it demonstrates the characteristics shared by all iterative processes. B/C/D are false claims about SIP. (IntroSwProcesses p.7)

questions.stem: Repeated changes under SIP are the basis of which software activities?
questions.explanation: Repeated changes underpin software evolution and servicing/reengineering. The other options are unrelated to the deck's claim. (IntroSwProcesses p.7)

questions.stem: Why does even a solo programmer need a defined process, per the deck?

options.text: To meet obligations: fulfill promises, pay bills, plan the future, and manage their own resources
Because a manager forces them to
There is no reason; solo work needs no process

questions.explanation: Even solo, a programmer must meet obligations (promises, bills, planning, resource management), which SIP enables, rather than just reacting ad hoc. B/C/D miss the deck's rationale. (IntroSwProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: Which are workproducts of SIP, according to the deck?

options.text: Product backlog, software code, and software documentation

questions.explanation: SIP's workproducts are the product backlog, the software code, and software documentation. B/C/D are not the deck's listed workproducts. (IntroSwProcesses p.9)

questions.stem: What does the product backlog represent in SIP?

options.text: The vision for the future of the software (bugs, new demands, and ideas)

questions.explanation: The product backlog is the change requests that represent the vision for the software's future, including bugs and new demands/ideas. B/C/D are different artifacts. (IntroSwProcesses p.9)

questions.stem: In the SIP model loop, what is the correct flow once an iteration backlog is drawn?

options.text: Software changes, then Code update, then Baseline, then Iteration/release, then Delivery to users
Delivery, then Baseline, then Software changes, then Code update
Baseline, then Delivery, then Iteration backlog, then Software changes
Code update, then Software changes, then Delivery, then Baseline

questions.explanation: Sol performs software changes, updates code, baselines, produces an iteration/release, then delivers to users, and the loop repeats. The other orderings scramble these steps. (IntroSwProcesses p.10)

questions.stem: In the SIP model, where do new change requests originate?

options.text: From users, flowing into the product backlog

questions.explanation: Change requests flow from users into the product backlog, from which an iteration backlog is drawn. The other sources are not part of the SIP model loop. (IntroSwProcesses p.10)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, which phase produces the comment 'Estimated set has 4 classes'?
questions.explanation: The estimated set with 4 classes is the output of IA. CL located CashierRecord; Act replaces the class; Base produces the baseline. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, the 'CL' step lands on which class?
questions.explanation: Concept location lands on CashierRecord. Session is the class extracted in Ref and replaced in Act; Item and Price belong to the IA example, not this CL step. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, the step 'Ref - Extract class Session' demonstrates that refactoring happens where relative to actualization?

options.text: Before actualization (prefactoring), preparing the code to receive the change

questions.explanation: Ref (extract class Session) precedes Act (replace class Session), i.e. prefactoring prepares a clean landing site before the actual change. B/C/D contradict the table's ordering. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, what does the 'Ex - Install new version of Bugzilla' entry represent?

options.text: An exception/interruption that deviates from the model
A normal phase of every software change

questions.explanation: The Bugzilla install is an exception/interruption, illustrating that enactment includes deviations. It is not a standard change phase, nor CL/Verification. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, what is the comment recorded at the 'Base' (baseline) step?
questions.explanation: At baseline, 2 regression faults are found and added to the backlog. The 4-class estimate is IA; extract Session is Ref; Add Cashier Session is Ini. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: Why does the SIP enactment table list IA as task 4, after CL and before Ref/Act?

options.text: Because Impact Analysis is one phase among the eight, sitting right after Concept Location and before Refactoring/Actualization
Because IA is always the last step
Because the table is in alphabetical order

questions.explanation: The table mirrors the change-process spine: IA follows CL and precedes Ref/Act. It is not last (B), does not replace CL (C), and is not alphabetical (D). (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: Why does the deck say process data should be collected?

options.text: Data indicate how the process is working and serve as the foundation for future planning
Data are required only for legal compliance
Data replace the need for a process model
Data are collected only after the project ends

questions.explanation: Data show how the process is working and ground future planning. B/C/D are not the deck's rationale. (IntroSwProcesses p.12)

questions.stem: What does a SIP time log record for each step?

options.text: Start/End time, number and minutes of interruptions, the step, and comments

questions.explanation: The time log records start/end, interruptions (count and minutes), the step (Pri/Ini/CL/IA/Ref/Ex/Act/Base), and comments. B/C/D are not what the time log captures. (IntroSwProcesses p.13)

questions.stem: What does 'total time' include, according to the deck?

options.text: Only the first hour of each step

questions.explanation: Total time includes all interruptions; clean time is the focused work time. B describes clean time; C/D are wrong. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: What is the formula for clean time?

options.text: Clean time = end - start - time of interruptions
Clean time = end - start + time of interruptions

questions.explanation: Clean time = end - start - interruptions. B adds interruptions (wrong sign); C gives total time; D is nonsensical. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: Which analogy does the deck use to explain clean time?

options.text: American football: 15 minutes is the clean time of each quarter, though total elapsed time is much longer
A marathon run at constant pace
A chess clock with no pauses

questions.explanation: The deck uses American football: each quarter's clean (game) time is 15 minutes even though the quarter takes much longer in real time. B/C/D are not the deck's analogy. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: An IA step runs 8:52 to 9:23 with 12 minutes of interruptions. What is its clean time?
questions.explanation: Clean time = 9:23 - 8:52 - 12 = 31 - 12 = 19 minutes. 31 is total time (B); 43 adds instead of subtracts (C); 12 is just the interruptions (D). (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: What kinds of summaries can be extracted from the raw time log?

options.text: Weekly clean-time summary, average time for concept location, and whether CL is getting faster or slower
The number of CI servers needed

questions.explanation: From the log you can derive weekly clean time, average concept-location time, trends in CL speed, and recurring SIP exceptions. B/C/D are not derivable from the time log. (IntroSwProcesses p.15)

questions.stem: A student computes a step's clean time as 'end minus start' only. What did they get wrong?

options.text: They computed total time; clean time must also subtract interruptions
They added interruptions instead of subtracting
They used the wrong start time

questions.explanation: end - start alone is total time; clean time subtracts interruptions. B is false; C/D describe different errors than the one made. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: What is the most commonly used measure of program size, despite being very inaccurate?
questions.explanation: LOC is the most commonly used size measure but is very inaccurate. Function points and class counts are alternatives; defect density measures quality, not size. (IntroSwProcesses p.16)

questions.stem: Why is LOC considered inaccurate as a size measure?

options.text: Different languages and coding styles give different sizes for the same functionality
It is impossible to count lines
It only works for compiled languages
It always overestimates by exactly 10 percent

questions.explanation: LOC varies with language and style, so the same functionality yields different counts. B/C/D are not the deck's reason. (IntroSwProcesses p.16-17)

questions.stem: How many significant digits of a LOC measure are meaningful, per the deck?

options.text: Only the one or two most significant digits (e.g. 900 LOC, 23 KLOC, 3.2 MLOC)

questions.explanation: Only the one or two most significant digits are meaningful, so you write 23 KLOC, not 23,418 LOC. B/C overstate precision; D understates LOC's usefulness. (IntroSwProcesses p.16-17)

questions.stem: How do function points compare to LOC, according to the deck?

options.text: They correlate with LOC but are harder to compute
They are unrelated to LOC and trivial to compute
They always equal LOC divided by 1000
They are the most accurate size measure known

questions.explanation: Function points correlate with LOC but are harder to compute. B/C/D mischaracterize the relationship. (IntroSwProcesses p.18)

questions.stem: Which size measures does the deck describe as EVEN LESS accurate than LOC?

options.text: Number of methods, number of classes, number of files

questions.explanation: Counting methods, classes, or files is even less accurate than LOC. Function points correlate with LOC; clean/total time and defect density measure other things. (IntroSwProcesses p.18)

questions.stem: How does the deck characterize code defects?
questions.explanation: Defects are incorrect computations and premature termination. B/C/D are style or performance concerns, not the deck's definition of defects. (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: What is the approximate defect density of good-quality software?
questions.explanation: Good-quality software is about 2.0 defects/KLOC. 0.1/KLOC is avionics (the cutting edge); 20 and 0.001 are not the deck's figures. (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: What defect density does the deck cite for avionics software at the cutting edge of what is achievable?
questions.explanation: Avionics (e.g. NASA Space Shuttle) reaches about 0.1 defects/KLOC. 2.0 is good general software; zero is unattainable in the deck; 1.0 is not cited. (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: A student writes that good software has ~0.1 defects/KLOC and avionics has ~2.0 defects/KLOC. What is wrong?

options.text: The figures are swapped: good software is ~2.0/KLOC and avionics is ~0.1/KLOC
Defect density is not measured per KLOC

questions.explanation: The numbers are reversed: good general software is ~2.0/KLOC and avionics ~0.1/KLOC. B is wrong; the deck gives distinct values (C wrong); defect density is per KLOC (D wrong). (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: Which example of cutting-edge avionics quality does the deck mention?
questions.explanation: The deck cites NASA Space Shuttle software as an avionics example reaching ~0.1 defects/KLOC. The other options are not cited. (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: What does a defect log record for each defect?

options.text: Found (date/time/task), location, description, origin (date/task introduced), and fixed date

questions.explanation: The defect log records when/where it was found, its location and description, its origin (date/task introduced), and when it was fixed. B/C/D are not the log's fields. (IntroSwProcesses p.20)

questions.stem: What is the main analytical value of recording a defect's 'origin' task in the defect log?

options.text: It connects a symptom back to the phase that introduced it, supporting process improvement
It assigns blame to a specific developer for payroll
It computes the LOC of the file

questions.explanation: Recording origin links the symptom to the phase that introduced it, enabling process improvement. B/C/D are not the deck's purpose. (IntroSwProcesses p.20)

questions.stem: In the defect-log example, a non-terminating loop in Cashier.get() is found during CL but its origin is recorded as which task?
questions.explanation: The example records the non-terminating loop found during CL but originating in Act. The Price=0 exception originates in Ref; the missing pop-up is found during Base; these are distinct rows. (IntroSwProcesses p.20)

questions.stem: In the SIP loop, where do the regression faults found at baseline ultimately flow?

options.text: Into the defect log and the product backlog, feeding the next iteration
Into the build server's cache only

questions.explanation: Baseline regression faults go into the defect log and the product backlog, feeding the next iteration and closing the SIP loop. B/C/D are incorrect. (IntroSwProcesses p.20, p.9)

questions.stem: How does the deck describe the relationship between recording the past and planning the future?

options.text: They are closely related; data about the past are good predictors and the future can't be predicted with certainty without knowing the past
The past determines the future with full certainty

questions.explanation: Planning is prediction under uncertainty; past data are good predictors and recording the past is tightly linked to planning the future. B/C ignore the link; D overstates certainty. (IntroSwProcesses p.21)

questions.stem: Which tasks are easiest to predict, according to the planning slides?
questions.explanation: Repetitions are the easiest to predict; unique, unprecedented tasks are hard to plan. B/C/D are the hard-to-predict cases. (IntroSwProcesses p.22)

questions.stem: Which proverb does the deck use to stress the repetitive nature of the process?

options.text: Repetition is the mother of skill
If it hurts, do it more often
Good process leads to good product

questions.explanation: 'Repetition is the mother of skill' stresses the value of repeatable tasks. 'If it hurts, do it more often' and 'keep it fast' are CI slogans; 'good process...' is the why-process slogan. (IntroSwProcesses p.22)

questions.stem: What are the two estimation techniques the deck names?
questions.explanation: The two techniques are analogy (from similar past phases) and decomposition (sum per-phase estimates). The other pairs belong to CI or quality methods, not estimation. (IntroSwProcesses p.23)

questions.stem: What does the 'analogy' estimation technique do?

options.text: Estimates a phase's time from similar past phases
Breaks the change into phases and sums their estimates

questions.explanation: Analogy estimates from similar past phases. Decomposition is the break-into-phases-and-sum approach (B); C/D are unrelated. (IntroSwProcesses p.23)

questions.stem: What is a noted advantage of the decomposition estimation technique?

options.text: Errors in per-phase estimates may compensate each other
It needs no past data at all
It ignores the phases of the change

questions.explanation: Decomposition breaks the change into phases and sums them, and the per-phase errors may compensate one another. B/C/D mischaracterize it. (IntroSwProcesses p.23)

questions.stem: What is the goal of 'tasking' in planning?

options.text: Make changes more alike and keep a narrow range of size so they're more predictable
Maximize the size of each change
Assign tasks to the most senior developer

questions.explanation: Tasking makes changes more alike with a narrow size range, improving predictability; large changes (epics) are divided. B/C/D are not tasking's goal. (IntroSwProcesses p.24)

questions.stem: In planning vocabulary, what is an 'epic'?

options.text: A large change that should be divided into smaller ones

questions.explanation: An epic is a large change to be divided into smaller, more uniform changes. B/C/D are unrelated concepts. (IntroSwProcesses p.24)

questions.stem: The 'Customers can download and use sales coupons online' example illustrates which planning idea?

options.text: Decomposing an epic into smaller changes (web site, coupon creation/removal, cashing at payment, usage database)
Computing clean time from a time log

questions.explanation: The coupons example decomposes a large epic into smaller changes. B/C/D are different topics. (IntroSwProcesses p.25)

questions.stem: What does the deck recommend about scheduling baselines?

options.text: Schedule them at regular intervals; postponing is not recommended
Postpone them until the end of the project
Baseline only when a bug is found

questions.explanation: Baselines should occur at regular intervals (e.g. end of shift), and postponing is not recommended. B/C/D contradict the advice. (IntroSwProcesses p.26)

questions.stem: What concern does the release plan primarily involve?

options.text: Business considerations, e.g. releasing certain new functionality on a certain date

questions.explanation: The release plan involves business considerations: promising certain functionality by a date, and planning makes that promise realistic. B/C/D are not release-planning concerns. (IntroSwProcesses p.27)

questions.stem: What is a main goal of planning with respect to risk?

options.text: Eliminating risk and uncertainty, emphasizing the repetitive nature of the process

questions.explanation: Planning aims to eliminate risk/uncertainty, leaning on repetition. B/C/D contradict the deck. (IntroSwProcesses p.22)

questions.stem: Why are unique, unprecedented tasks hard to plan, per the deck's logic?

options.text: They lack the past data and repetition that make prediction reliable
They are always larger than epics

questions.explanation: Prediction relies on past data and repetition; unprecedented tasks lack both, so they're hard to plan. B/C/D are unrelated claims. (IntroSwProcesses p.22)

questions.stem: What does a release backlog table list and snapshot?

options.text: Each planned change as a row, with the effort remaining for each, snapshotted after x hours of work
The Agile Manifesto values over time

questions.explanation: A release backlog table has changes as rows and remaining-effort columns snapshotted over hours of work. B/C/D are not what the table shows. (IntroSwProcesses p.28)

questions.stem: In the original release backlog (12 changes), what is the remaining effort and total effort to reach the goal?

options.text: Remaining effort 330, total effort to goal 330
Remaining effort 440, total effort to goal 330
Remaining effort 0, total effort to goal 475
Remaining effort 340, total effort to goal 440

questions.explanation: The original table shows remaining effort 330 and total effort to goal 330. 440 and 340 appear after 100 hours; 0/475 is the late-release endpoint. (IntroSwProcesses p.28)

questions.stem: After 100 hours, the table shows remaining effort 340 and total effort to goal 440. What does this combination indicate?

options.text: 100 hours were done but remaining effort went up, so the goal moved as estimates grew
The release is exactly on schedule
All estimates shrank during the work

questions.explanation: Remaining effort rose from 330 to 340 despite 100 hours of work, and total-to-goal climbed to 440 because estimates grew (inventory 30->50, cashier sessions 35->80, etc.). The goal moved; the release is not on schedule or early. (IntroSwProcesses p.29)

questions.stem: In the 'late release' scenario, the remaining-effort row goes 330, 340, 180, 70, 0 across snapshots. What does reaching 0 at 475 hours mean?

options.text: The release ships, but later than originally planned (475h vs the original 330)
The release shipped on the original schedule

questions.explanation: Remaining effort drives to 0 but only at 475 hours, versus the planned 330 - a late but complete release. It was not cancelled (B) or on schedule (C); dropping changes is the incomplete-release scenario (D). (IntroSwProcesses p.30)

questions.stem: In the 'incomplete release' scenario, what happens to items 11 (credit payment) and 12 (check payment)?

options.text: They are left below the cut line; the release ships at 405 hours without them
They are completed first, before all other items
They are the only items shipped

questions.explanation: In the incomplete release, items 11 and 12 fall below the cut and the release goes out at 405 hours without them (70 of effort un-shipped). B/C/D misread the scenario. (IntroSwProcesses p.31)

questions.stem: Which two bottom rows of a release backlog table should you watch, and what do their trends mean?

options.text: Remaining effort falling means you're converging; total effort to reach the goal rising means scope/estimates are inflating
Remaining effort rising is good; total effort to goal falling is bad
Both rows should always be equal

questions.explanation: Remaining-effort trending to 0 signals convergence; a rising total-effort-to-goal signals scope/estimate inflation, letting you spot a slipping release early. B inverts the meaning; C/D are false. (IntroSwProcesses p.28-31)

questions.stem: Between the original and after-100-hours snapshots, which estimate grew the most?
questions.explanation: Cashier sessions grew 35 to 80 (a jump of 45), the largest increase. Inventory rose 20, multiple prices 10, multiple line items 35. (IntroSwProcesses p.29)

questions.stem: How can you tell from a release backlog table that a release will be late rather than incomplete?

options.text: Remaining effort eventually reaches 0 (all changes done) but only after more hours than originally planned
Some changes are left below the cut line and never reach 0
The total effort to goal stays constant
The table has only one column

questions.explanation: A late release still drives remaining effort to 0 (everything ships) but takes more hours than planned. An incomplete release leaves items below the cut, un-shipped. C/D are not diagnostic signs. (IntroSwProcesses p.30-31)

questions.stem: In the late-release scenario, at which snapshots is remaining effort recorded?
questions.explanation: The late-release table snapshots at 0/100/285/405/475 hours. The other column sets do not match the deck. (IntroSwProcesses p.30)

questions.stem: Why do programmers organize into teams, according to the Team Processes deck?

options.text: Most projects need more effort than a solo programmer can handle
Teams remove the need for any process

questions.explanation: Teams form because most projects exceed what one programmer can do. B/C/D are false. (TeamProcesses p.2)

questions.stem: Which best describes the AIP (Agile Iterative Process)?

options.text: Agile process for small-to-medium teams; decisions by consensus; no specializations; only the programmer role
Manager-directed with several specialized roles, scaling to large teams
Adds a permission-to-commit safeguard via architects
A solo process for one programmer

questions.explanation: AIP is agile, small/medium, consensus-driven, with no specialization (only the programmer role). B is DIP, C is CIP, D is SIP. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: What is AIP's micro-cycle?
questions.explanation: AIP's micro-cycle is DEVELOP -> REACT -> MODIFY. Commit/build/test is the CI cycle; plan/do/check and initiate/locate/analyze are not AIP's cycle. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: In the AIP model, where does the iteration backlog come from?

options.text: It is extracted from the product backlog
It is generated by the build server
It is written by the architect

questions.explanation: The iteration backlog is extracted from the product backlog. The build server, architect, and defect log do not produce it. (TeamProcesses p.5-6)

questions.stem: What two extra roles are attached to the backlog/meetings in the AIP model?

options.text: A Product Manager and a Process Manager
An Architect and a Code Owner
A Tester and a Build Engineer

questions.explanation: AIP attaches a Product Manager and a Process Manager to the backlog/meetings. Architect/Code Owner appear as safeguards in CIP; the others aren't named for AIP. (TeamProcesses p.5)

questions.stem: What does the AIP iteration meeting accomplish?

options.text: Assesses the current product state with all stakeholders and plans the next iteration, producing an iteration backlog from the product backlog

questions.explanation: The iteration meeting assesses product state (technical + business viewpoints) and plans the next iteration's backlog. B/C/D are other activities. (TeamProcesses p.6)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a stated function of the AIP daily meeting?

options.text: Granting permission to commit via architects and code owners
Giving early warning when anything goes wrong

questions.explanation: Permission-to-commit is the CIP safeguard, not a daily-meeting function. The daily meeting does assign change requests, identify refactoring needs, and give early warnings. (TeamProcesses p.7, p.16)

questions.stem: Which of these does the AIP daily meeting do?

options.text: Surfaces daily problems, builds consensus on progress, assigns change requests, and clarifies ambiguities

questions.explanation: The daily meeting surfaces problems, builds consensus, assigns change requests daily, clarifies ambiguities, and flags refactoring needs. B/C/D are not its role. (TeamProcesses p.7)

questions.stem: In AIP, how are programmers' tasks structured with respect to specialization?

options.text: There are no specializations; developers have only the programmer role
Each developer has a distinct specialized role

questions.explanation: AIP has no specializations - developers hold only the programmer role. B/C/D describe directed or safeguarded processes, not AIP. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: In AIP, how are decisions made?
questions.explanation: AIP decisions are made by consensus. Management directive characterizes DIP; the architect and build server do not decide in AIP. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: In what year was the Agile Manifesto developed?
questions.explanation: The Agile Manifesto was developed in 2001 by 17 original authors. 1991 is when Booch coined CI; 1999 is Beck's XP book; 2012 is the Rajlich textbook copyright. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: How many original authors developed the Agile Manifesto?
questions.explanation: The Manifesto had 17 original authors. 4 is the number of value statements; 12 and 21 are not the deck's figures. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: Which is one of the four Agile Manifesto value statements?

options.text: Following a plan over responding to change

questions.explanation: 'Working software over comprehensive documentation' is a Manifesto value. B/C/D reverse the actual statements. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: Which is the FIRST Agile Manifesto value statement?

options.text: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Processes and tools over individuals and interactions

questions.explanation: 'Individuals and interactions over processes and tools' is the first Manifesto value. B/C/D invert the actual statements. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: Which pair correctly completes two of the Agile Manifesto values?

options.text: Customer collaboration over contract negotiation; Responding to change over following a plan
Contract negotiation over customer collaboration; Following a plan over responding to change
Tools over individuals; Documentation over software
Plan over change; Contract over collaboration

questions.explanation: The values are customer collaboration over contract negotiation and responding to change over following a plan. The other options reverse them. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: What does the Agile Manifesto mean by valuing the items on the left 'over' those on the right?

options.text: The left items are valued more, while the right items still have value
The right items are valued more

questions.explanation: 'X over Y' means X is valued more, but while there is value in the items on the right, the left is valued more. The right items are not worthless (B), not valued more (C), and not nonexistent (D). (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: Which best describes the DIP (Directed Iterative Process)?

options.text: Runs under the direction of managers, with several specialized programmer roles, scaling to large teams and systems
Consensus-driven with no specialization, small/medium teams
A single programmer adding functionality one step at a time
Open-source development with a wide community

questions.explanation: DIP is manager-directed with specialized roles and scales large. A is correct; B is AIP; C is SIP; D is open source. (TeamProcesses p.10)

questions.stem: In the DIP model, who handles the Build step?
questions.explanation: In DIP, testers handle the build; developers run parallel software changes; process managers run the iteration review/release. Users and product managers feed the backlog. (TeamProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: What is the Architect's role in a directed process?

options.text: Guarantees developers preserve architecture constraints and approves or disapproves commits

questions.explanation: The architect preserves architecture constraints and approves/disapproves commits. B/C/D are not the architect's stated duties. (TeamProcesses p.13)

questions.stem: What is the difference between a product manager and a process manager?

options.text: Product managers make strategic decisions; process managers assign tasks and control the process
Product managers write tests; process managers write code
Product managers run the build; process managers approve commits

questions.explanation: Product managers make strategic decisions; process managers assign tasks and control the process. B/C/D mischaracterize the split. (TeamProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: What is the testers' role in DIP?
questions.explanation: Testers verify the new baseline. Commit approval is the architect's job; strategy is the product manager's; backlog extraction happens at the iteration meeting. (TeamProcesses p.12-13)

questions.stem: What does the deck say about specialization in directed processes?

options.text: Specialization increases effectiveness, and additional specialized roles are possible
Only one role is ever allowed

questions.explanation: The deck states specialization increases effectiveness and allows additional specialized roles. B/C/D contradict it. (TeamProcesses p.12)

questions.stem: What is the distinguishing feature of the CIP (Centralized/Safeguarded Iterative Process) versus DIP?

options.text: An explicit 'permission to commit' gate controlled by architects and code owners
It is consensus-driven with no managers

questions.explanation: CIP adds a 'permission to commit' gate granted by architects and code owners - its signature versus DIP. B/C/D are false. (TeamProcesses p.15-16)

questions.stem: In CIP, before developers commit, whose permission must they obtain?
questions.explanation: CIP requires permission to commit from architects and code owners. The product manager handles strategy; users don't gate commits; D describes an unsafeguarded process. (TeamProcesses p.16)

questions.stem: Which one-line comparison of AIP, DIP, and CIP is correct?

options.text: AIP = consensus/no roles; DIP = manager-directed/specialized; CIP = DIP plus a commit safeguard
AIP = manager-directed; DIP = consensus; CIP = solo
AIP = commit safeguard; DIP = no roles; CIP = consensus

questions.explanation: AIP is consensus with no specialization; DIP is manager-directed with specialized roles; CIP is DIP plus the permission-to-commit safeguard. B/C scramble these; D is false. (TeamProcesses p.4, p.10, p.16)

questions.stem: What is the downside of code ownership noted by the deck?
questions.explanation: With code ownership, programmers specialize in parts of the code and coordination can become a problem. B/C are overstated; D ignores the stated risk. (TeamProcesses p.17)

questions.stem: How does the deck characterize Open Source Development?

options.text: Safeguarded, using code ownership, with a wide community of developers of variable skills
Solo work by a single owner

questions.explanation: Open source is safeguarded, uses code ownership, and has a wide community of variable skill. B/C/D contradict the deck. (TeamProcesses p.18)

questions.stem: How does CI's stance on code ownership contrast with the team-processes notion of code ownership?

options.text: CI says the team owns the code, not the individual, whereas code ownership has programmers specialize in parts of the code
CI requires individual ownership of every file
Code ownership means no one owns the code

questions.explanation: CI holds that the team owns the code (not the individual), contrasting with code ownership where programmers specialize in parts of the code. B/C/D misstate the contrast. (ContinuousIntegration p.5; TeamProcesses p.17)

questions.stem: How does the deck define Continuous Integration?

options.text: Teams integrate their work multiple times per day, each integration verified by an automated build
Teams integrate once at the end of the project
Each developer keeps a private branch indefinitely
Integration is done manually by a release manager monthly

questions.explanation: CI means integrating many times per day with each integration verified by an automated build. B/C/D describe the opposite of CI. (ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: What benefit does CI provide, per the deck?

options.text: It significantly reduces integration problems and lets you develop cohesive software more rapidly
It removes the need for any tests

questions.explanation: CI significantly reduces integration problems and speeds cohesive development. B/C/D are false claims. (ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: What is the CI cycle shown in the deck's diagram?

options.text: Commit, then Build, then Test, then Report, back to Development, around source control
Initiation, then Concept Location, then Impact Analysis

questions.explanation: The CI cycle is Commit -> Build -> Test -> Report -> back to Development around source control. DEVELOP/REACT/MODIFY is AIP's cycle; the others are change-process phases. (ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: Who first proposed the term 'Continuous Integration', and in what year?
questions.explanation: Booch first proposed the term CI in his 1991 method (though he did not advocate integrating several times a day). Beck/XP later advocated frequent integration; the Manifesto and Rajlich are different. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: What is the key nuance about Booch and CI in the CI lab history?

options.text: Booch coined 'CI' but did NOT advocate integrating several times a day
Booch coined CI and advocated tens of integrations per day

questions.explanation: Booch coined the term but did not advocate daily integration; it was XP/Beck who advocated integrating more than once per day. B/C/D misstate the history. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: Which approach adopted CI and advocated integrating more than once per day - perhaps tens of times per day?
questions.explanation: XP (Beck) adopted CI and advocated integrating many times per day. Booch coined the term but didn't advocate daily integration; waterfall and SIP are unrelated. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: How does the CI lab define CI in historical terms?

options.text: Merging all developers' working copies to a shared mainline several times a day
Keeping each developer's copy isolated until release
Manual QA at the end of each month

questions.explanation: CI is merging all working copies to a shared mainline several times a day. B/C/D describe non-CI practices. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: Which is the FIRST of the five CI principles listed in the deck?
questions.explanation: The five principles begin with 'Environments based on stability.' The others are also principles but not the first listed. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: Which of these is one of the five CI principles?

options.text: Commit frequently and build every commit
Commit only at the end of each iteration

questions.explanation: 'Commit frequently and build every commit' is a core CI principle. B/C/D contradict the principles. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: Which set lists CI principles, all five of which appear in the deck?

options.text: Environments based on stability; maintain a code repository; commit frequently and build every commit; make the build self-testing; keep the build fast
Write documentation first; integrate monthly; use private branches; skip tests; deploy manually
Pair programming; stand-up meetings; burndown charts; retrospectives; velocity

questions.explanation: A lists exactly the five CI principles. B inverts them; C lists agile ceremonies; D lists OO concepts. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: What does the principle 'Environments based on stability' describe?

options.text: Create server environments that model code stability and promote code to stricter environments as quality improves (Dev to Test to Stage to Prod)
Run everything on a single production server

questions.explanation: This principle promotes code through Dev -> Test -> Stage -> Prod as quality improves. B/C/D contradict the promotion pipeline. (ContinuousIntegration p.4)

questions.stem: What is the classic promotion pipeline implied by 'environments based on stability'?

options.text: Dev to Test to Stage to Prod
Prod to Stage to Test to Dev
Initiation to Concept Location to Impact Analysis

questions.explanation: Code is promoted to stricter environments: Dev -> Test -> Stage -> Prod. B reverses it; C is part of the CI cycle; D is the change process. (ContinuousIntegration p.4)

questions.stem: Which three references ground the CI lab's history (CI definition, Booch, and XP)?

options.text: Beck, Rajlich, and the Agile Manifesto

questions.explanation: The CI lab cites [Tho] for the CI definition, [Boo] for Booch coining CI, and [Bec99] for XP adopting it. Rajlich is the change-process textbook, not the CI history source here. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: Under 'commit frequently / build every commit', what habits does the deck urge?

options.text: Commit small, functional changes; write unit tests; remember the team owns the code, not the individual
Commit huge batches once a week; skip unit tests
Let each individual own their code permanently

questions.explanation: The deck urges small functional commits, unit tests, and that the team (not the individual) owns the code. B/C/D contradict the principle. (ContinuousIntegration p.5)

questions.stem: The phrase 'the code builds on my box' is used in the deck to highlight which anti-pattern?

options.text: Relying on a developer's local machine instead of the repository and build server as the source of truth
Promoting code through too many environments

questions.explanation: 'Builds on my box' is the anti-pattern CI exists to defeat: the repo is the source of record and the build server settles disputes. B/C/D are not the point. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: What is the source-code repository's role in CI, per the deck?

options.text: It is the source of record
It is only a backup of the developer's laptop

questions.explanation: The repository is the source of record in CI. B/C/D understate or misstate its role. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: What makes the build server the 'final authority on stability/quality'?

options.text: It only gets code from the repository, so it settles disputes that 'it works on my machine' cannot
It runs on the most powerful developer's laptop
It can override the repository contents
It is maintained by the product manager

questions.explanation: The build server pulls only from the repo and is therefore the neutral final authority, killing the 'works on my machine' excuse. B/C/D are incorrect. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: Which agile principle justifies 'build every commit'?

options.text: If it hurts, do it more often
Repetition is the mother of skill
Good process leads to good product

questions.explanation: 'If it hurts, do it more often' justifies building every commit - difficult activities get easier with frequency. The other slogans are real but justify different points. (ContinuousIntegration p.7)

questions.stem: Beyond making integration a habit, why does building every commit help quality?

options.text: It reduces the time between defect introduction and removal
It increases the number of branches
It removes the need for the repository
It eliminates the need for code review
