questions.explanation: Frequent builds shrink the gap between introducing and removing a defect, which is why the build is automated. B/C/D are not the stated rationale. (ContinuousIntegration p.7)

questions.stem: What conclusion does 'why build every commit' reach about the build?
questions.explanation: The slide concludes you should automate the build so it can run on every commit. B/C/D contradict this. (ContinuousIntegration p.7)

questions.stem: How efficient are individual programmers at finding their OWN bugs, per the CI deck?
questions.explanation: Individuals are less than 50% efficient at finding their own bugs, which is why multiple methods are needed. B/C/D overstate individual efficiency. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: How many quality methods does the CI deck say are needed for greater than 90% defect removal?
questions.explanation: Using 3 or more methods achieves greater than 90% defect removal, because multiple methods discover more defects. B/C understate; D overstates. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: Which does the CI deck name as the MOST effective defect-discovery methods?

options.text: Design inspections, code inspections, and testing

questions.explanation: The most effective methods are design inspections, code inspections, and testing. B/C/D are not the deck's named most-effective methods. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: Why does the CI deck recommend multiple quality methods rather than relying on one?

options.text: Multiple quality methods discover more defects than any single method
Multiple methods are cheaper than one

questions.explanation: Multiple methods discover more defects, since individuals are under 50% efficient alone. B contradicts the data; C/D are not stated. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: According to the deck, why are unit tests the best method for verifying builds?

options.text: They are fast (no database or file system), focused (pinpoint problems)
They are slow but thorough end-to-end checks
They require a full production environment

questions.explanation: Unit tests are fast (no I/O) and focused, making them best for verifying builds. End-to-end/slow describes system tests (B); C/D are false. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: How do system tests differ from unit tests in the CI deck's self-testing-build discussion?

options.text: System tests are end-to-end and often take minutes to hours; unit tests are fast and focused
System tests are faster than unit tests
Unit tests are end-to-end; system tests are focused

questions.explanation: System tests are end-to-end and slow (minutes to hours); unit tests are fast and focused. B/C reverse the properties; D is false. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: What is a 'self-testing build' in CI?

options.text: A build that runs its own tests (especially unit tests) and is the best method for verifying builds
A build that skips all tests for speed
A build that only compiles, never tests
A build run only on the developer's laptop

questions.explanation: A self-testing build runs its own tests (especially unit tests) and is the best way to verify a build. B/C remove testing; D misplaces it. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: In CI's automated-quality slide, what does static code analysis do?

options.text: Looks for common Java bugs (Findbugs, PMD) and checks code compliance (Checkstyle)
Promotes code from Dev to Prod

questions.explanation: Static analysis finds common Java bugs (Findbugs, PMD) and checks compliance (Checkstyle). Coverage is measured by Cobertura (B); system tests and promotion are different (C/D). (ContinuousIntegration p.10)

questions.stem: In the automated-quality slide, which tool is named for measuring unit-test coverage?
questions.explanation: Cobertura measures coverage. Findbugs and PMD find bugs; Checkstyle checks compliance. (ContinuousIntegration p.10)

questions.stem: What does the SONAR tool find in CI's automated-quality slide?

options.text: Hotspots of low testing plus high complexity
The exact LOC of each file
The clean time of each step

questions.explanation: SONAR finds hotspots combining low testing with high complexity. B/C/D are unrelated to SONAR's role here. (ContinuousIntegration p.10)

questions.stem: Which tool is named for checking code compliance (style) in the deck?
questions.explanation: Checkstyle checks code compliance/style. Cobertura and Emma measure coverage; JUnit is a test framework. (ContinuousIntegration p.10)

questions.stem: On the build-server hardware slide, which activity is said to need 'lots and lots' of CPU?
questions.explanation: Static analysis needs lots and lots of CPU; compile plus unit test needs lots of CPU; Maven plus Java needs lots of memory. Committing and logging are not CPU-heavy here. (ContinuousIntegration p.11)

questions.stem: On the build-server hardware slide, what needs lots of memory?
questions.explanation: Maven plus Java needs lots of memory. The other tasks are not the deck's memory-heavy items. (ContinuousIntegration p.11)

questions.stem: Which recurring imperative appears on the build-server hardware slide?
questions.explanation: The recurring imperative is 'Please, KEEP IT FAST.' The other phrases are not on the slide. (ContinuousIntegration p.11)

questions.stem: Which of these is listed as a test framework in the CI deck?
questions.explanation: JUnit is a test framework (with NUnit, MSTest, Selenium, FitNesse). Cobertura is coverage; Checkstyle and Findbugs are static analysis. (ContinuousIntegration p.12)

questions.stem: Which set are all named as test frameworks in the deck?
questions.explanation: A lists the test frameworks. B lists static-analysis tools; C lists coverage tools; D mixes build tools and a tracker. (ContinuousIntegration p.12)

questions.stem: Which set are all named as static-analysis tools in the deck?

options.text: Checkstyle, CodeScanner, DRY, Crap4j, Findbugs, PMD, Fortify, Sonar, FXCop

questions.explanation: A is the static-analysis list. B is test frameworks; C is coverage tools; D is the environment pipeline. (ContinuousIntegration p.13)

questions.stem: Which set are all named as code-coverage tools in the deck?
questions.explanation: A lists coverage tools. B is test frameworks; C is static analysis; D is build/CI infrastructure. (ContinuousIntegration p.14)

questions.stem: In the coverage-view example, what do green and red lines indicate, and what does '1 of 2 branches missed' mean?

options.text: Green = covered lines, red = uncovered lines; one of two branches was not exercised by tests
Green = passing tests, red = failing tests; two branches both failed
Green = new code, red = deleted code
Green = high complexity, red = low complexity

questions.explanation: In a coverage view, green lines are covered, red are uncovered, and '1 of 2 branches missed' means one branch was not exercised by tests. B/C/D misread the coverage view. (ContinuousIntegration p.14)

questions.stem: Which two tools are named as examples of static analysis that looks for common Java bugs?
questions.explanation: Findbugs and PMD look for common Java bugs. Cobertura/Emma are coverage; JUnit/Selenium are test frameworks; Maven/Java are build tooling. (ContinuousIntegration p.10)

questions.stem: In the CI lab, which CI infrastructure is set up?

options.text: GitHub Actions (a .yml workflow) plus a Maven build

questions.explanation: The CI lab stands up GitHub Actions with a .yml workflow and a Maven build. The other CI/build combinations are not what the lab uses. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: In the CI lab, what is the .maven-settings.xml in the project root used for?

options.text: To consume shared jars from GitHub Packages

questions.explanation: The .maven-settings.xml lets the build consume shared jars from GitHub Packages. B/C/D are not its purpose. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: In the CI lab, the workflow is configured to build on what event?

options.text: Only on a manual trigger once a month
Only when a release is tagged

questions.explanation: The lab configures the workflow to build on every pull request with Maven and to run tests automatically. B/C/D contradict the lab's 'build every commit' realization. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: Which two CI principles does the CI lab pipeline most directly realize?

options.text: Build every commit and make the build self-testing
Environments based on stability and keep it cheap
Maintain a code repository and write documentation first

questions.explanation: Building on every PR and running tests automatically realizes 'build every commit' and 'make the build self-testing.' B/C/D are not what the pipeline demonstrates. (CILab p.1; ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: What does Impact Analysis determine about a change?

options.text: The strategy and impact of the change
The salary cost of the change
The marketing plan for the change
The exact compile time of the change

questions.explanation: IA determines the strategy and impact of change. B/C/D are not what IA computes. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: Which set does Impact Analysis produce?
questions.explanation: IA produces the estimated impact set. The initial impact set is its input; the backlogs are process workproducts. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: In the interaction graph G = (X, I), what does X stand for?
questions.explanation: X is the set of classes (vertices); I is the set of interactions (edges). The other readings are wrong. (ImpactAnalysis p.6)

questions.stem: Which mark does a propagating ('mailman') class receive during Impact Analysis?
questions.explanation: A mailman class is marked PROPAGATING. UNCHANGED would wrongly stop propagation; BLANK/NEXT are pre-inspection states. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Which kind of interaction is directional (one class depends on another via a contract)?
questions.explanation: A dependency is the directional, contract-based interaction. Coordination is shared coding/dataflow; neighborhood and propagation are different notions. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: In the temperature change example, what conversion is requested?
questions.explanation: The example changes the display from Fahrenheit to Celsius. The other conversions are not the deck's example. (ImpactAnalysis p.14)

questions.stem: In the granularity hierarchy, what example is given for the 'process' level?
questions.explanation: SIP/AIP/DIP exemplify the process level. Staged/waterfall are lifecycles; class inspection is a step; evolution/servicing are stages. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: What does SIP stand for?
questions.explanation: SIP is the Solo Iterative Process. The other expansions are invented. (IntroSwProcesses p.7)

questions.stem: What does AIP stand for?
questions.explanation: AIP is the Agile Iterative Process. The other expansions are invented. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: What does DIP stand for?
questions.explanation: DIP is the Directed Iterative Process. The other expansions are invented. (TeamProcesses p.10)

questions.stem: What is the focused work time in a SIP time log called?
questions.explanation: Clean time is the focused work time; total time includes interruptions. Cycle/lead time are not the deck's terms. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: Which workproduct of SIP represents the vision for the future of the software?
questions.explanation: The product backlog represents the vision for the software's future. The defect/time logs are measurements; a baseline is an integrated version. (IntroSwProcesses p.9)

questions.stem: How many original authors signed the Agile Manifesto, and in which year was it created?
questions.explanation: 17 authors created the Agile Manifesto in 2001. The other pairings are wrong. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: How often does CI have teams integrate their work?

options.text: Only at the end of the project

questions.explanation: CI integrates work multiple times per day, each verified by an automated build. B/C/D describe non-CI cadences. (ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: In CI, what is the final authority on stability and quality?
questions.explanation: The build server is the final authority on stability/quality and only gets code from the repo. The laptop is the anti-pattern; the others are unrelated. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: Which CI principle concerns build speed?
questions.explanation: 'Keep the build fast' is the speed principle. The others address repository, testing, and environments. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: Which CI principle requires the build to run its own tests?
questions.explanation: 'Make the build self-testing' requires the build to run its own tests. The others are different principles. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: Which kind of test is fast, focused, and uses no database or file system?
questions.explanation: Unit tests are fast, focused, and avoid I/O, making them best for verifying builds. System/acceptance/manual tests are slower or broader. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: Which CI slogan justifies building on every commit?

options.text: If it hurts, do it more often
Repetition is the mother of skill
Good process leads to good product

questions.explanation: 'If it hurts, do it more often' justifies building every commit. The others are real slogans but justify other points. (ContinuousIntegration p.7)

questions.stem: Approximately what defect density characterizes good-quality software?
questions.explanation: Good software is about 2.0 defects/KLOC; ~0.1/KLOC is avionics. 10 and 0 are not the deck's figures. (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: What does CIP add that DIP lacks?
questions.explanation: CIP adds a permission-to-commit safeguard via architects and code owners. Daily meetings, backlogs, and iteration meetings appear across the processes. (TeamProcesses p.16)

questions.stem: In the AIP micro-cycle DEVELOP, REACT, MODIFY, which step comes first?
questions.explanation: The AIP micro-cycle is DEVELOP -> REACT -> MODIFY, so DEVELOP is first. COMMIT belongs to the CI cycle. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: Which tool is an example of a code-coverage tool in the CI deck?
questions.explanation: Emma is a coverage tool. PMD is static analysis; JUnit and Selenium are test frameworks. (ContinuousIntegration p.14)

questions.stem: Which slogan summarizes the motivation for studying software processes?

options.text: Good process leads to good product

questions.explanation: 'Good process leads to good product' is the deck's slogan. The others are not from the deck. (IntroSwProcesses p.1)

questions.stem: Mapping the SIP enactment table to the eight-phase spine, which sequence of abbreviations corresponds to Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, and Actualization in order?
questions.explanation: The spine runs Concept Location (CL), Impact Analysis (IA), Prefactoring/refactoring (Ref), then Actualization (Act). The other orders scramble the phases. (IntroSwProcesses p.11; ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: Why does the granularity table tie the whole course together with respect to Impact Analysis?

options.text: It shows a software change is a task, concept location and actualization are phases, class inspection is a step, and SIP/AIP/DIP are the processes that repeat the change task
It proves Impact Analysis is a lifecycle model
It shows that all phases have the same granularity
It replaces the eight-phase change process

questions.explanation: The table places change=task, phases=concept location/actualization, step=class inspection, processes=SIP/AIP/DIP, locating IA as a phase of class-inspection steps. B/C/D misread the hierarchy. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: A team integrates many times a day, builds every commit, self-tests the build, keeps it fast, and treats the repo and build server as the source of truth. Which practice is being followed?
questions.explanation: These are exactly CI's core behaviours. SIP is solo; code ownership is about specialization; waterfall is a lifecycle. (ContinuousIntegration p.2-11)

questions.stem: On an interaction graph A-B-C-D (a path), if A is the only CHANGED class and B is found PROPAGATING while C is UNCHANGED, which classes end up in the estimated impact set?
questions.explanation: The estimated set is CHANGED plus PROPAGATING = {A, B}. B being PROPAGATING schedules C, but C is UNCHANGED so it neither joins the set nor expands to D; D is never reached. (ImpactAnalysis p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: Suppose Concept Location returns an empty initial impact set (no seed class). What does the IA marking algorithm produce?

options.text: An empty estimated impact set, because there is no CHANGED seed to schedule any NEXT neighbors
The entire graph, because all classes default to CHANGED
An error that halts the build

questions.explanation: With no seed CHANGED class, no neighbors are scheduled NEXT, so the loop ends immediately with an empty estimated set. The algorithm never defaults classes to CHANGED (B), invents PROPAGATING marks (C), or throws build errors (D). (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: A release backlog shows remaining effort reaching 0 only at 475 hours (planned 330) with all 12 changes shipped. How should this release be classified?
questions.explanation: All changes ship (remaining effort hits 0) but only at 475h versus a planned 330h, so it is late but complete. On-time (B) is wrong; nothing was dropped so it is not incomplete (C); it shipped so not cancelled (D). (IntroSwProcesses p.30)

questions.stem: Across the four process forms, which statement is FALSE?

options.text: Performance is the blueprint of how tasks fit together
Enactment includes inevitable deviations from the model
The plan is the expected future performance
The model is a prescription of the tasks

questions.explanation: Performance is the set of measures collected, not the blueprint; the blueprint is the model. B, C, and D correctly describe enactment, plan, and model. (IntroSwProcesses p.5-6)

questions.stem: Which statement about AIP, DIP, and CIP is FALSE?

options.text: AIP scales to the largest teams and systems via specialized roles
DIP runs under the direction of managers with specialized roles
AIP makes decisions by consensus with no specialization

questions.explanation: AIP is for small-to-medium teams with no specialization; it is DIP that scales large via specialized roles. B, C, and D are all true. (TeamProcesses p.4, p.10, p.16)

questions.stem: Combining the CI deck's figures, why is a self-testing build with multiple quality methods recommended over trusting the author's own checking?

options.text: Individuals are under 50% efficient at finding their own bugs, but 3+ methods reach over 90% defect removal
Authors find 100% of their own bugs, so methods are redundant
A single method already removes over 90% of defects
Self-testing builds remove the need for any inspection

questions.explanation: Because individuals are under 50% efficient alone, combining 3 or more methods (inspections + testing) reaches over 90% defect removal. B/C contradict the figures; D ignores that inspections remain a top method. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: Why must Impact Analysis use the interaction graph rather than the dependency graph, even though dependencies are easier to extract automatically?

options.text: Change propagates bidirectionally, so a one-directional dependency graph would miss impacts that ripple back up a dependency
Dependency graphs cannot be drawn for Java programs
Interaction graphs are always smaller and faster to traverse

questions.explanation: Interactions are undirected because change flows both ways; a dependency graph followed one direction would miss back-propagation. B/C/D are false. (ImpactAnalysis p.5, p.9)

questions.stem: Where does Impact Analysis sit in the eight-phase change process?

options.text: Third, after Concept Location and before Prefactoring

questions.explanation: IA is the third phase: Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, and so on. B/C/D misplace it. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, which abbreviation denotes the impact-analysis task?
questions.explanation: IA denotes impact analysis. CL is concept location, Act is actualization, Base is baseline. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: Why does the deck say only the one or two most significant digits of a LOC figure are meaningful?

options.text: Because LOC is very inaccurate, varying with language and style
Because computers cannot count beyond two digits
Because LOC is always rounded down by law

questions.explanation: LOC's inaccuracy (it varies by language and style) means only the top one or two digits carry meaning, e.g. 23 KLOC. B/C/D are false. (IntroSwProcesses p.16-17)

questions.stem: What does promoting code to 'stricter environments as quality improves' mean in CI?

options.text: Code advances through Dev, Test, Stage, and Prod as it proves more stable
Code is deleted as it improves
Code is committed less often as it improves
Code skips testing once it reaches Stage

questions.explanation: Stricter environments form the Dev -> Test -> Stage -> Prod promotion pipeline, advancing code as stability improves. B/C/D contradict the principle. (ContinuousIntegration p.4)
